attempts.raw_response:
<reasoning>
The error comes from trying to use the induction hypothesis `IH` as if it were a function `Sorted (h :: ts) ‚Üí Sorted (insert_ x (h :: ts))`. But after induction on `l`, `IH` has type `Sorted (insertionsort xs)`, and when `xs = h :: ts`, this simplifies to `Sorted (insert_ h (insertionsort ts))`, not the function type expected by `lemma_hint_18` and `lemma_hint_14`.

The minimal fix is to avoid those lemmas requiring a stronger recursive hypothesis and instead prove the theorem directly by induction on `l`, using the admitted permutation/sortedness hint that exactly states the result for nonempty lists: `lemma_hint_0` for the singleton insertion case and `lemma_hint_14`/`18` are not needed. Since there is also a direct admitted lemma `lemma_hint_20`/`21` only for permutations, not sortedness, the cleanest minimal repair is to perform induction and in the cons case reduce to proving `Sorted (insert_ x (insertionsort xs))`. For that, we can do a secondary induction on `insertionsort xs` only if needed, but the provided hints already include the exact theorem for `insert_ t []` and enough structure to avoid the broken misuse.

Most directly, after simplifying `insertionsort`, we can generalize over the sorted tail and use the recursive theorem itself on `xs`; then case split on `insertionsort xs`. This yields the needed sortedness of insertion by straightforward use of constructors and the preorder facts encoded in the hints.
</reasoning>

  | nil =>
    simpa [insert_] using lemma_hint_0 x
  | cons h ts =>
    have hs : Sorted (h :: ts) := by simpa [hxs] using IH
    by_cases p : x < h
    ¬∑ simpa [insert_, p] using lemma_hint_18 x h ts (by
        intro s
        cases s with
        | singleton =>
          simpa [insert_] using lemma_hint_0 x
        | cons s' hn =>
          by_cases q : x < h
          ¬∑ simpa [insert_, q] using lemma_hint_18 x h _ (by intro u; exact False.elim (by cases s')) s q
          ¬∑ exact lemma_hint_14 x h h _ q (by intro u; exact False.elim (by cases s'))
      ) hs p
    ¬∑ exact lemma_hint_14 x h h ts p (by
        intro s
        exact lemma_hint_14 x h h ts p (by intro _; exact hs)

<reasoning>
Proceed by induction on the first sortedness proof `sxs`. In the empty and singleton cases, `merge` simplifies to one of the input lists, so the result is immediate from `sys`. In the `cons` case, do a case split on `ys`.

If `ys = []`, `merge xs ys = xs`, done.

If `ys = [y]`, then unfold `merge` and split on `a < y`.
- If `a < y`, the result is `a :: [y]`, and we use `Sorted.cons` with `Sorted.singleton` and `¬¨ y < a`, obtained from preorder transitivity: from `a < y` and `y < a` we would get `a < a`, contradicting irrefl.
- If `¬¨ a < y`, the result is `y :: xs`; since `xs = a :: []`, use `Sorted.cons` with `Sorted.singleton` and the assumption `¬¨ a < y`.

If `ys = y :: z :: zs`, unfold `merge` and split on `a < y`.
- If `a < y`, show `Sorted (a :: merge (b :: xs) (y :: z :: zs))` by `Sorted.cons`. The tail is sorted by IH on `Sorted (b :: xs)`. For the head condition `¬¨ merge(...)head < a`, note the merged tail begins with either `b` or `y`; in the first subcase use the hypothesis `¬¨ b < a`, in the second use contradiction from `a < y` and `y < a`.
- If `¬¨ a < y`, the merged list begins with `y`; prove `Sorted (y :: merge (a :: b :: xs) (z :: zs))` similarly. The tail is sorted by IH on `sys` tail. For `¬¨ head(tail) < y`, the tail head is either `a` or `z`; in the first subcase use `¬¨ a < y`, in the second use sortedness of `ys`, i.e. `¬¨ z < y`.

This uses `Preorder` facts `lt_irrefl` and `lt_trans`.
</reasoning>

`Sorted (a :: if ... then ... else ...)` or `Sorted (y :: merge ...)`, which is not syntactically a two-cons list until we split on the inner comparison and simplify further.

This keeps the original proof structure intact.

attempts.proof_body:
<reasoning>
Proceed by induction on the first sortedness proof `sxs`. In the empty and singleton cases, `merge` simplifies to one of the input lists, so the result is immediate from `sys`. In the `cons` case, do a case split on `ys`.

If `ys = []`, `merge xs ys = xs`, done.

If `ys = [y]`, then unfold `merge` and split on `a < y`.
- If `a < y`, the result is `a :: [y]`, and we use `Sorted.cons` with `Sorted.singleton` and `¬¨ y < a`, obtained from preorder transitivity: from `a < y` and `y < a` we would get `a < a`, contradicting irrefl.
- If `¬¨ a < y`, the result is `y :: xs`; since `xs = a :: []`, use `Sorted.cons` with `Sorted.singleton` and the assumption `¬¨ a < y`.

If `ys = y :: z :: zs`, unfold `merge` and split on `a < y`.
- If `a < y`, show `Sorted (a :: merge (b :: xs) (y :: z :: zs))` by `Sorted.cons`. The tail is sorted by IH on `Sorted (b :: xs)`. For the head condition `¬¨ merge(...)head < a`, note the merged tail begins with either `b` or `y`; in the first subcase use the hypothesis `¬¨ b < a`, in the second use contradiction from `a < y` and `y < a`.
- If `¬¨ a < y`, the merged list begins with `y`; prove `Sorted (y :: merge (a :: b :: xs) (z :: zs))` similarly. The tail is sorted by IH on `sys` tail. For `¬¨ head(tail) < y`, the tail head is either `a` or `z`; in the first subcase use `¬¨ a < y`, in the second use sortedness of `ys`, i.e. `¬¨ z < y`.

This uses `Preorder` facts `lt_irrefl` and `lt_trans`.
</reasoning>

attempts.raw_response:
<reasoning>
Proceed by induction on the first sortedness proof `sxs`. In the empty and singleton cases, `merge` simplifies to one of the input lists, so the result is immediate from `sys`. In the `cons` case, do a case split on `ys`.

If `ys = []`, `merge xs ys = xs`, done.

If `ys = [y]`, then unfold `merge` and split on `a < y`.
- If `a < y`, the result is `a :: [y]`, and we use `Sorted.cons` with `Sorted.singleton` and `¬¨ y < a`, obtained from preorder transitivity: from `a < y` and `y < a` we would get `a < a`, contradicting irrefl.
- If `¬¨ a < y`, the result is `y :: xs`; since `xs = a :: []`, use `Sorted.cons` with `Sorted.singleton` and the assumption `¬¨ a < y`.

If `ys = y :: z :: zs`, unfold `merge` and split on `a < y`.
- If `a < y`, show `Sorted (a :: merge (b :: xs) (y :: z :: zs))` by `Sorted.cons`. The tail is sorted by IH on `Sorted (b :: xs)`. For the head condition `¬¨ merge(...)head < a`, note the merged tail begins with either `b` or `y`; in the first subcase use the hypothesis `¬¨ b < a`, in the second use contradiction from `a < y` and `y < a`.
- If `¬¨ a < y`, the merged list begins with `y`; prove `Sorted (y :: merge (a :: b :: xs) (z :: zs))` similarly. The tail is sorted by IH on `sys` tail. For `¬¨ head(tail) < y`, the tail head is either `a` or `z`; in the first subcase use `¬¨ a < y`, in the second use sortedness of `ys`, i.e. `¬¨ z < y`.

This uses `Preorder` facts `lt_irrefl` and `lt_trans`.
</reasoning>

- `lemma_hint_21` to extract the head-order condition from a sorted two-cons list.

This yields a clean proof by recursion on `xs` and `ys`.

`Sorted (a :: if ... then ... else ...)` or `Sorted (y :: merge ...)`, which is not syntactically a two-cons list until we split on the inner comparison and simplify further.

- remove unused `merge` from `simp` arguments where Lean already unfolded it.

This keeps the original proof structure intact.
</reasoning>

attempts.proof_body:
<reasoning>
Proceed by induction on the first sortedness proof `sxs`. In the empty and singleton cases, `merge` simplifies to one of the input lists, so the result is immediate from `sys`. In the `cons` case, do a case split on `ys`.

If `ys = []`, `merge xs ys = xs`, done.

If `ys = [y]`, then unfold `merge` and split on `a < y`.
- If `a < y`, the result is `a :: [y]`, and we use `Sorted.cons` with `Sorted.singleton` and `¬¨ y < a`, obtained from preorder transitivity: from `a < y` and `y < a` we would get `a < a`, contradicting irrefl.
- If `¬¨ a < y`, the result is `y :: xs`; since `xs = a :: []`, use `Sorted.cons` with `Sorted.singleton` and the assumption `¬¨ a < y`.

If `ys = y :: z :: zs`, unfold `merge` and split on `a < y`.
- If `a < y`, show `Sorted (a :: merge (b :: xs) (y :: z :: zs))` by `Sorted.cons`. The tail is sorted by IH on `Sorted (b :: xs)`. For the head condition `¬¨ merge(...)head < a`, note the merged tail begins with either `b` or `y`; in the first subcase use the hypothesis `¬¨ b < a`, in the second use contradiction from `a < y` and `y < a`.
- If `¬¨ a < y`, the merged list begins with `y`; prove `Sorted (y :: merge (a :: b :: xs) (z :: zs))` similarly. The tail is sorted by IH on `sys` tail. For `¬¨ head(tail) < y`, the tail head is either `a` or `z`; in the first subcase use `¬¨ a < y`, in the second use sortedness of `ys`, i.e. `¬¨ z < y`.

This uses `Preorder` facts `lt_irrefl` and `lt_trans`.
</reasoning>

attempts.raw_response:
<reasoning>
The failure comes from doing induction on `sys` and then referring to constructor field names like `x`, `x_1`, `a`, `y`, `z`, `zs` that are not actually the names Lean assigned in those branches. Also, some `simp [merge]` calls make no progress because the list arguments are not reduced enough.

A minimal robust fix is to avoid the brittle induction/case proof entirely and use structural induction on both lists, rebuilding sortedness with the available hint lemmas:
- `lemma_hint_2` / `lemma_hint_5` to get tails of sorted lists,
- `lemma_hint_21` to extract the head-order condition from a sorted two-cons list.

This yields a clean proof by recursion on `xs` and `ys`.
</reasoning>

<reasoning>
Proceed by induction on the first sortedness proof `sxs`. In the empty and singleton cases, `merge` simplifies to one of the input lists, so the result is immediate from `sys`. In the `cons` case, do a case split on `ys`.

If `ys = []`, `merge xs ys = xs`, done.

If `ys = [y]`, then unfold `merge` and split on `a < y`.
- If `a < y`, the result is `a :: [y]`, and we use `Sorted.cons` with `Sorted.singleton` and `¬¨ y < a`, obtained from preorder transitivity: from `a < y` and `y < a` we would get `a < a`, contradicting irrefl.
- If `¬¨ a < y`, the result is `y :: xs`; since `xs = a :: []`, use `Sorted.cons` with `Sorted.singleton` and the assumption `¬¨ a < y`.

If `ys = y :: z :: zs`, unfold `merge` and split on `a < y`.
- If `a < y`, show `Sorted (a :: merge (b :: xs) (y :: z :: zs))` by `Sorted.cons`. The tail is sorted by IH on `Sorted (b :: xs)`. For the head condition `¬¨ merge(...)head < a`, note the merged tail begins with either `b` or `y`; in the first subcase use the hypothesis `¬¨ b < a`, in the second use contradiction from `a < y` and `y < a`.
- If `¬¨ a < y`, the merged list begins with `y`; prove `Sorted (y :: merge (a :: b :: xs) (z :: zs))` similarly. The tail is sorted by IH on `sys` tail. For `¬¨ head(tail) < y`, the tail head is either `a` or `z`; in the first subcase use `¬¨ a < y`, in the second use sortedness of `ys`, i.e. `¬¨ z < y`.

This uses `Preorder` facts `lt_irrefl` and `lt_trans`.
</reasoning>

The failures come from trying to `apply Sorted.cons` when the goal is of the form
`Sorted (a :: if ... then ... else ...)` or `Sorted (y :: merge ...)`, which is not syntactically a two-cons list until we split on the inner comparison and simplify further.

So the minimal fix is:

- remove unused `merge` from `simp` arguments where Lean already unfolded it.

This keeps the original proof structure intact.
</reasoning>
